# -*- sh -*-
# See http://rigaux.org/language-study/diagram.html for the format of this file
#claude: auto-generated by Claude Code (inspired from my other lineage.txt)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# C/C++ compilers lineage (mostly)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#src: wikipedia articles on different programs
#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_compilers
#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_compiler_construction

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pre-Historical
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Fortran compiler (1957) is the first real optimizing compiler
# but out of scope here

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Historical (UNIX)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Dennis Ritchie's first C compiler was a two-pass compiler
# that generated PDP-11 assembly

*cc
  1973
  SY:2
  # Dennis Ritchie, first C compiler, UNIX V4
  # rewrote B compiler into C compiler

*pcc
  1979
  cc
  SY:2
  # Steve Johnson, Portable C Compiler
  # made C portable across architectures, used in UNIX V7 and BSD

*ccom
  1978
  cc
  SY:2
  # the actual compiler pass in UNIX V7 (cc is the driver)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (GNU)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*gcc
  1987
  pcc
  SY:2
  # Richard Stallman, first release 1987 (C only)
  # gcc date: 1.0 released March 1987
gcc 2.0
  1992
  # C++ support (g++)
*gcc 3.0
  2001
  # SSA-based optimization, tree-ssa merge
*gcc 4.0
  2005
  # Tree SSA, autovectorization
gcc 4.5
  2010
  # link-time optimization (LTO)
*gcc 5.0
  2015
gcc 10
  2020

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (LLVM)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*LLVM
  2003
  SY:2
  # Chris Lattner, UIUC, originally a research project
  # modular compiler infrastructure, reusable passes

*Clang
  2007
  LLVM
  SY:2
  # C/C++/Objective-C frontend for LLVM
  # much better error messages than gcc
Clang 3.0
  2011
  # C++11 support
Clang 6.0
  2018

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Plan9
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*2c/5c/8c/...
  1993
  cc
  SY:1
  # Ken Thompson, very fast, simple register allocator
  # no preprocessor (separate cpp), no optimizer
Inferno compilers
  1996

*Go compiler
  2009
  2c/5c/8c/...
  # originally in C (derived from Plan 9 compilers), rewritten in Go 2015

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Educational
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*lcc
  1991
  # Chris Fraser and David Hanson, retargetable C compiler
  # companion book "A Retargetable C Compiler" (1995)

*Small C
  1980
  cc
  # Ron Cain, subset C compiler, published in Dr. Dobb's
  # very influential for teaching compiler construction

*TCC
  2001
  lcc
  # Fabrice Bellard, Tiny C Compiler
  # compiles C almost instantly, self-hosting, ~30K LOC

*subc
  2004
  Small C
  # Nils M. Holm, book "SubC: A Simple C Compiler"

*8cc
  2012
  # Rui Ueyama, small self-hosting C compiler
  # later wrote chibicc

chibicc
  2020
  8cc
  # Rui Ueyama, educational C compiler, excellent code

cproc
  2019
  # Michael Forney, C11 compiler using QBE backend

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Industry
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

# ------------
# Microsoft
# ------------

*Microsoft C
  1983
  SY:6
  # Microsoft C Compiler, later became part of Visual Studio

QuickC
  1987
  Microsoft C
  SY:6
  # lightweight, fast compilation, integrated environment

*Visual C++
  1993
  Microsoft C
  SY:6
  # part of Visual Studio, cl.exe
Visual C++ 6.0
  1998
Visual C++ 2005
  2005
  # C99 partial support

*MSVC
  2015
  Visual C++
  SY:6
  # rebranded, part of Visual Studio 2015+, improved C++ standards conformance

# ------------
# Borland
# ------------

*Turbo C
  1987
  SY:6
  # Borland, very fast compilation, integrated IDE
Turbo C++
  1990
  SY:6

*Borland C++
  1991
  Turbo C++
  SY:6
Borland C++ 5
  1996

C++Builder
  1997
  Borland C++
  SY:6

# ------------
# Intel
# ------------

*ICC
  1999
  SY:6
  # Intel C++ Compiler, best x86 optimization

ICX
  2020
  ICC,LLVM
  SY:6
  # Intel oneAPI DPC++/C++, LLVM-based replacement for ICC

# ------------
# Other industry
# ------------

*DJGPP
  1989
  gcc
  SY:6
  # DJ Delorie, gcc port for DOS (32-bit protected mode via DPMI)

Watcom C
  1988
  SY:6
  # excellent optimizer for its era, used in game development
Open Watcom
  2002

Metrowerks CodeWarrior
  1993
  SY:6
  # Mac/embedded, very fast compilation

*ARM Compiler
  1993
  SY:6
  # armcc, later versions (6+) LLVM-based

Green Hills
  1983
  SY:6
  # embedded/safety-critical compilers

*ACK
  1983
  pcc
  SY:6
  # Amsterdam Compiler Kit, Andrew Tanenbaum
  # multi-frontend, multi-backend (table-driven)
ACK open source
  2003

Wind River / Diab
  1986
  SY:6
  # embedded C/C++ compiler

HP aCC
  1996
  SY:6
  # HP C++ compiler for HP-UX

Sun Studio / Oracle Developer Studio
  1994
  SY:6
  # Forte, then Sun Studio, then Oracle Developer Studio

IBM XL C
  1990
  SY:6
  # for AIX/z/OS, very strong optimization

MIPSpro
  1992
  SY:6
  # SGI, for IRIX, excellent optimization

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Cross / Embedded
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*SDCC
  1999
  gcc
  # Small Device C Compiler, 8-bit targets (8051, Z80, PIC, etc.)

*cc65
  1989
  # Ullrich von Bassewitz, C compiler for 6502
  # used with ca65 assembler

avr-gcc
  1999
  gcc
  # gcc port for Atari AVR microcontrollers

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Compiler backends / infrastructure
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

QBE
  2015
  # Quentin Carbonneaux, simple optimizing backend
  # designed as a lightweight alternative to LLVM

*Cranelift
  2017
  # Bytecode Alliance, Rust, used as backend for Wasmtime and rustc (experimental)

MLIR
  2019
  LLVM
  # Multi-Level IR, part of LLVM project
  # Chris Lattner, for domain-specific compilers

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# C++ specific (standards-driven)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*cfront
  1983
  cc
  # Bjarne Stroustrup, first C++ compiler, C++-to-C translator
cfront 2.0
  1989
cfront 3.0
  1991

*Comeau C++
  2001
  cfront
  # most conformant C++ compiler for a long time, based on Edison Design Group frontend

*EDG (Edison Design Group)
  1988
  cfront
  SY:6
  # C++ frontend used by many commercial compilers (Intel, Comeau, etc.)
